# Reclaim the unused Chroma FTS5 index (~495MB) — no full-text consumer; governed drop, Chroma-tolerance V-B-A first

Sub of #14079 (Memory Core Chroma 2.5GB bloat). Scopes the FTS-index slice with a confirmed finding + the one open safety question.

## Confirmed finding (V-B-A 2026-06-27)

**No Neo query path uses Chroma full-text.** A repo sweep of `ai/services/` + `ai/mcp/server/` for `$contains` / `whereDocument` / `where_document` / `fts5` / full-text returns **zero** consumers — every MC and KB query is vector/semantic. So the per-collection FTS5 index Chroma auto-builds for document substring search is **dead weight** (~495MB of the bloat).

## The open safety question (do this V-B-A before any drop)

Chroma **auto-maintains** the FTS5 tables as part of its internal schema — so "no Neo consumer" is necessary-but-not-sufficient. Before dropping anything, verify Chroma **tolerates** FTS-table removal:
- Does Chroma rebuild the FTS index on the next `add` (making the drop futile / churny)?
- Does a query/add **error** if the fts tables are absent (Chroma assuming its own schema invariant)?
- Is there a Chroma collection-creation **config** that disables FTS at build-time (the clean path), vs a post-hoc table-drop (the risky path)?

## Implementation (gated on the tolerance V-B-A)

If Chroma tolerates it: a **governed** (ADR-0027 envelope — dry-run default, backup, exclusive-access window like `defragChromaDB`) drop of the unused fts5 tables + `VACUUM` to reclaim the freed pages. If Chroma rebuilds/requires them: a Chroma-version/config approach (disable FTS at collection creation) instead, or accept the index as a Chroma cost and close this slice as won't-fix with the rationale.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Chroma-tolerance V-B-A documented (rebuild? error-on-absent? config-to-disable?).
- [ ] If safely droppable: governed drop + VACUUM reclaims the FTS pages with zero query/add regression (a behavior test over the live collections post-drop).
- [ ] If not: documented won't-fix with the Chroma-internal-dependency rationale.

Refs #14079 (parent bloat epic). Sibling slices: the field↔document de-dup (~910MB, consumer-aware refactor), the SQLite VACUUM (~191MB), the abandoned-shadow promotion.

---
🤖 Authored by **Ada** (@neo-opus-ada · Claude Opus 4.8, Claude Code). FTS-consumer V-B-A confirmed; Chroma-tolerance V-B-A pending.

## Timeline

- 2026-06-27T03:12:45Z @neo-opus-ada cross-referenced by #14193
### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-27T03:22:20Z

## FTS index MEASURED: ~556MB dead weight + a 3rd document copy

Read-only `dbstat` on the live `.neo-ai-data/chroma/unified/chroma.sqlite3`:
- `embedding_fulltext_search_data`: **392 MB** (the FTS5 index)
- `embedding_fulltext_search_content`: **163 MB** (a COPY of the document text)
- `_docsize`/`_idx`/`_config`: ~1.3 MB

**~556 MB total** — larger than the ~495MB estimate. And `_content` is a **third** copy of the turn text (alongside `chroma:document` + the split metadata fields) → directly compounds #14193's de-dup.

## Chroma-tolerance — confirmed NOT naively droppable

These are Chroma's standard FTS5 virtual-table shadow tables (`embedding_fulltext_search` is an FTS5 vtable). Chroma writes them on every `add` for `$contains`, so a manual drop would error on the next `add`. Safe paths to verify (next V-B-A): (a) a chromadb collection-creation config to DISABLE the FTS index; (b) a content-less/metadata-only collection mode; (c) else it's a Chroma-internal cost → upstream issue or won't-fix. The measurement confirms the prize (~556MB, ~22% of the 2.5GB) is worth the V-B-A. — Ada

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-27T03:23:15Z

## chromadb 3.3.1: no standalone FTS-disable → the FTS reclaim is a CONSEQUENCE of #14193's de-dup direction

chromadb `^3.3.1` (package.json); `createCollection(options)` (ChromaManager:161). chromadb 3.x has **no** 'disable FTS' config — the FTS5 index exists BECAUSE Neo stores `documents` in Chroma (Chroma auto-indexes documents for `$contains`; `embedding_fulltext_search_content` literally IS the document copy).

**So #14192 ⇄ #14193 are COUPLED:** if #14193's de-dup picks the **split metadata fields** as canonical and STOPS passing `documents` to Chroma `add()` (turn text in metadata only), Chroma builds NO FTS index → the ~556MB FTS + the chroma:document copy are BOTH reclaimed in one move. No documents in Chroma → no FTS, no `_content`.

**Implication:** the FTS reclaim is NOT a standalone 'drop the index' task (chromadb won't allow that safely) — it's the downstream benefit of the de-dup choosing split-fields-canonical. The consumers currently reading `include:['documents']` (#14193's list: MemoryService/SummaryService/SessionService/DatabaseService) must migrate to the split fields first. This likely **folds #14192's execution into #14193's canonical choice** — keeping #14192 for the FTS-specific measurement + as the reclaim-verification (post-de-dup: confirm Chroma rebuilds no FTS). — Ada
